systemNote: A birth chart places all three inner-life pillars in a sign, and they don't repeat the same meaning. The SUN sign describes core identity — ego, willpower, the basic self one is growing into; it's what people mean by 'my sign.' The MOON sign describes the inner and emotional nature — instinctive reactions, comfort needs, and how a person processes feeling, often operating beneath conscious awareness. The RISING sign (Ascendant) is the outward manner and first impression — the social mask, physical demeanor, and style through which the Sun and Moon get expressed to the world. Two people can share a Sun sign and seem completely different once Moon and Rising are factored in.

entries.name: Aries
entries.meaning: Aries is the first sign of the zodiac, marking the start of spring, and carries the energy of initiation and new beginnings. Ruled by Mars, it is bold, direct, and driven by a need to act and compete rather than deliberate. As a Cardinal Fire sign, Aries leads with instinct and courage, often diving into ventures headfirst, though this can come with impatience and a tendency to move on before finishing what was started.

entries.name: Taurus
entries.meaning: Taurus is ruled by Venus and values comfort, beauty, and material security. As a Fixed Earth sign, it is patient, reliable, and slow to change course, building things to last rather than chasing novelty. Taurus's grounded steadiness can shade into stubbornness or resistance to change, but it also gives the sign deep loyalty and an appreciation for the physical pleasures of life.

entries.name: Gemini
entries.meaning: Gemini is ruled by Mercury, the planet of communication, and is defined by curiosity, quick thinking, and a love of variety. As a Mutable Air sign, it adapts easily, moves fluidly between ideas and social circles, and thrives on mental stimulation and conversation. The classic 'twins' symbolism reflects Gemini's dual nature, which can bring versatility and wit but also restlessness and inconsistency.

entries.name: Cancer
entries.meaning: Cancer is ruled by the Moon and is deeply attuned to emotion, memory, and belonging. As a Cardinal Water sign, it initiates through nurturing and care, often building and protecting a home or family unit with strong instinct. Cancer's sensitivity gives it deep empathy and intuition, but it can also retreat into its shell or swing through moods when it feels unsafe.

entries.name: Leo
entries.meaning: Leo is ruled by the Sun and radiates warmth, confidence, and a desire to be seen and appreciated. As a Fixed Fire sign, it sustains its energy and passions steadily, expressing itself with creativity, generosity, and a natural flair for drama or performance. Leo's pride and need for recognition can tip into vanity or stubbornness, but at its best the sign is loyal, big-hearted, and inspiring to others.

entries.name: Virgo
entries.meaning: Virgo is ruled by Mercury and channels that mental energy into analysis, precision, and practical service rather than Gemini's scattered curiosity. As a Mutable Earth sign, it adapts through refinement, order, and attention to detail, often finding purpose in improving systems, health, or daily routines. Virgo's discernment makes it dependable and helpful, though it can slide into excessive self-criticism or nitpicking of others.

entries.name: Libra
entries.meaning: Libra is ruled by Venus and seeks balance, fairness, and harmony, especially within relationships and partnerships. As a Cardinal Air sign, it initiates through diplomacy and social connection, weighing multiple perspectives to reach an equitable outcome. Libra's appreciation for beauty and grace makes it charming and cooperative, but its constant weighing of options can lead to indecision or a tendency to avoid conflict.

entries.name: Scorpio
entries.meaning: Scorpio is ruled by Pluto in modern astrology (Mars traditionally) and is known for its emotional depth, intensity, and drive toward transformation. As a Fixed Water sign, it holds its feelings and convictions with great power and focus, often probing beneath the surface of people and situations. Scorpio's guardedness and need for control can produce secrecy or jealousy, but the same intensity gives it remarkable resilience and the ability to regenerate after crisis.

entries.name: Sagittarius
entries.meaning: Sagittarius is ruled by Jupiter, the planet of expansion, and is driven by a love of freedom, exploration, and the pursuit of meaning. As a Mutable Fire sign, it adapts by seeking new horizons, whether through travel, higher learning, or philosophy, and brings enthusiasm and optimism wherever it goes. Sagittarius's honesty and directness can come across as tactless, and its restlessness can make commitment or routine feel confining.

entries.name: Capricorn
entries.meaning: Capricorn is ruled by Saturn and is defined by discipline, ambition, and a long-term drive toward achievement and status. As a Cardinal Earth sign, it initiates through structure and hard work, methodically building toward goals with patience and pragmatism. Capricorn's seriousness and self-control earn it respect and reliability, though it can appear reserved, overly cautious, or preoccupied with duty.

entries.name: Aquarius
entries.meaning: Aquarius is ruled by Uranus in modern astrology (Saturn traditionally) and values independence, originality, and progress for the collective good. As a Fixed Air sign, it holds firmly to its own ideas and principles, often standing apart from convention to champion humanitarian causes or innovative thinking. Aquarius's individuality and vision make it a natural reformer, but the same detachment can read as aloof or emotionally distant.

entries.name: Pisces
entries.meaning: Pisces is ruled by Neptune in modern astrology (Jupiter traditionally) and is the most emotionally absorbent and imaginative sign of the zodiac, closing the cycle as the final sign. As a Mutable Water sign, it adapts by dissolving boundaries, empathizing deeply with others, and drawing on intuition, art, and spirituality. Pisces's compassion and imagination make it deeply creative and caring, but its dreaminess and difficulty with boundaries can lead to escapism or being easily overwhelmed by others' emotions.

systemNote: The Chinese zodiac assigns one of 12 animals to each lunar year in a fixed 12-year cycle, layered with one of 5 elements (Wu Xing) that each cycle through in a 60-year Stem-Branch combination — so a person's year animal is their public, outward self, while the element is the underlying material it is forged from and colors how that animal's traits are expressed. Chinese New Year falls in late January or February (not January 1), so anyone born before that year's lunar new year date technically belongs to the previous year's animal — a January or early-February birth should always be checked against the exact lunar boundary rather than assumed.

entries.name: Rat
entries.meaning: The Rat is the first animal of the cycle, associated with quick wit, charm, and resourcefulness. Rats are natural problem-solvers who are adaptable, sociable, and good at spotting opportunities others miss. The shadow side is a tendency toward opportunism, restlessness, and a scheming or acquisitive streak when insecure.

entries.name: Ox
entries.meaning: The Ox represents diligence, dependability, and quiet strength. Oxen are methodical, patient workers who value stability and earn trust through consistency rather than flash. Their shadow is stubbornness and rigidity — a reluctance to change course or hear other viewpoints once their mind is set.

entries.name: Tiger
entries.meaning: The Tiger symbolizes courage, competitiveness, and natural authority. Tigers are bold, charismatic risk-takers who attract followers and thrive on challenge. The cost is impulsiveness and a rebellious, sometimes reckless streak that can alienate more cautious people around them.

entries.name: Rabbit
entries.meaning: The Rabbit stands for gentleness, tact, and refined taste. Rabbits are diplomatic and empathetic, preferring peace and harmony and skilled at smoothing over conflict. Their shadow is excessive caution and conflict-avoidance, which can shade into passivity or a tendency to withdraw rather than confront problems.

entries.name: Dragon
entries.meaning: The Dragon is the only mythical animal in the cycle and is considered the most auspicious, symbolizing power, ambition, and charisma. Dragons are natural leaders with big visions and infectious confidence. The shadow is a domineering, impatient streak and a tendency toward arrogance when their high standards aren't met.

entries.name: Snake
entries.meaning: The Snake represents wisdom, intuition, and quiet strategic depth. Snakes are perceptive and elegant, preferring to observe and plan rather than act rashly, and they often possess sharp business or intellectual instincts. Their shadow is secretiveness and suspicion, sometimes curdling into jealousy or manipulation.

entries.name: Horse
entries.meaning: The Horse embodies energy, freedom, and an adventurous spirit. Horses are independent, sociable, and driven by movement — they dislike confinement and thrive when free to travel or change direction. The shadow is restlessness and impatience, which can look like fickleness or an inability to commit and finish what they start.

entries.name: Goat
entries.meaning: The Goat (also called Sheep or Ram) symbolizes gentleness, creativity, and compassion. Goats are artistic, nurturing, and sensitive to others' feelings, often gravitating toward aesthetic or caring pursuits. Their shadow is indecisiveness and a worry-prone, dependent streak that surfaces under pressure or uncertainty.

entries.name: Monkey
entries.meaning: The Monkey stands for cleverness, wit, and inventive problem-solving. Monkeys are versatile and quick-thinking, able to charm and improvise their way through almost any situation. The shadow is a mischievous, cunning streak — a taste for tricks and shortcuts that can tip into unreliability or deceit.

entries.name: Rooster
entries.meaning: The Rooster represents diligence, precision, and confident self-presentation. Roosters are observant, hardworking, and organized, often perfectionists who take visible pride in a job well done. Their shadow is a critical, vain streak that can come across as boastful or overly blunt toward others' shortcomings.

entries.name: Dog
entries.meaning: The Dog symbolizes loyalty, honesty, and a strong sense of justice. Dogs are protective and trustworthy, deeply devoted to the people and causes they commit to. The shadow is a tendency toward anxiety and stubborn pessimism, sometimes becoming overly guarded or suspicious of others' motives.

entries.name: Pig
entries.meaning: The Pig (also called Boar) represents generosity, honesty, and good-natured diligence. Pigs are warm and easygoing, quick to share and slow to hold grudges, often the most tolerant sign of the cycle. Their shadow is naivety and self-indulgence, leaving them vulnerable to being taken advantage of or overindulging in comfort.

entries.name: Wood
entries.meaning: Wood governs growth, planning, and upward expansion, corresponding to spring and new beginnings. A Wood-influenced personality tends to be idealistic, cooperative, and generous, with a natural drive to grow and help things flourish. Overextended, Wood people can become overly rigid in their ideals or spread themselves too thin chasing every new direction.

entries.name: Fire
entries.meaning: Fire governs passion, visibility, and dynamic action, corresponding to summer and peak energy. A Fire-influenced personality is warm, charismatic, and expressive, drawn to leadership and the spotlight. Its excess brings impulsiveness and volatility — a short temper or burnout from burning too brightly too fast.

entries.name: Earth
entries.meaning: Earth governs stability, nourishment, and the center that holds all other elements together, corresponding to the transitions between seasons. An Earth-influenced personality is practical, reliable, and nurturing, valuing security and steady routine over risk. Too much Earth produces stubbornness and an overcautious resistance to change.

entries.name: Metal
entries.meaning: Metal governs structure, discipline, and refinement, corresponding to autumn and the process of cutting away what is unnecessary. A Metal-influenced personality is precise, principled, and determined, with strong standards for themselves and others. In excess it hardens into rigidity, coldness, or an unforgiving, detached judgment of those who fall short.

entries.name: Water
entries.meaning: Water governs wisdom, adaptability, and depth, corresponding to winter and quiet introspection. A Water-influenced personality is intuitive, calm, and flexible, able to flow around obstacles rather than confront them directly. Its shadow is fearfulness and secretiveness, retreating into isolation or indecision when overwhelmed.

The 1 is the number of the leader and the pioneer, driven by independence, willpower, and the urge to originate rather than follow. It represents self-reliance, ambition, and the courage to stand alone and start new things. In its balanced form it is confident and decisive; out of balance it can tip into egotism, stubbornness, or domineering behavior.

The 2 is the number of partnership and diplomacy, concerned with cooperation, balance, and harmony between people. It is sensitive, receptive, and skilled at mediating, working best in relationships and teams rather than alone. Its shadow side shows up as indecision, excessive dependence on others, or oversensitivity to conflict.

The 3 is the number of creative self-expression, communication, and sociability, associated with optimism, humor, and artistic or verbal talent. It thrives on interaction and enjoys inspiring others, often through writing, speaking, or the arts. Unbalanced, it can scatter its energy, become superficial, or avoid emotional depth through distraction.

The 4 is the number of structure, discipline, and hard work, building solid foundations through patience and methodical effort. It values order, reliability, and practical results, making it the number of the builder and the organizer. Its excess brings rigidity, stubbornness, or an overly cautious resistance to change.

The 5 is the number of freedom, change, and adventure, restless for variety and resistant to routine or restriction. It is adaptable, curious, and drawn to travel, new experience, and sensory life. Out of balance it becomes scattered, impulsive, or irresponsible, chasing stimulation at the expense of commitment.

The 6 is the number of responsibility, care, and domestic harmony, centered on family, community, and service to others. It is nurturing, loyal, and drawn to healing or beautifying its surroundings, often taking on the role of caretaker. Its shadow is self-sacrifice taken too far, becoming controlling, martyred, or overly interfering in others' lives.

The 7 is the number of introspection, analysis, and the search for deeper truth, whether through study, spirituality, or solitary reflection. It is contemplative and perceptive, valuing knowledge and inner understanding over material or social concerns. Unbalanced, it can withdraw into isolation, aloofness, or excessive skepticism.

The 8 is the number of material achievement, ambition, and personal power, concerned with authority, finance, and large-scale accomplishment. It carries strong executive and organizational ability, oriented toward status and tangible results in business or leadership. Its excess brings materialism, workaholism, or a ruthless drive for control.

The 9 is the number of completion and universal compassion, oriented toward humanitarian ideals, generosity, and service to the greater good rather than personal gain. It closes cycles, synthesizing what came before and encouraging release and forgiveness. Out of balance it can become martyr-like, emotionally scattered, or prone to disappointment when its idealism meets reality.

The 11 is a master number of heightened intuition and spiritual insight, considered a more charged, higher-octave version of the 2. It carries visionary, inspirational energy and a strong sense of idealism, often functioning as a channel for illumination or inspiration to others. Its intensity brings great sensitivity and nervous tension, and unrealized it can reduce to the more grounded traits of the 2.

The 22 is the 'Master Builder' number, combining the visionary idealism of 11 with the practical discipline of 4 to turn big dreams into large-scale, lasting achievement. It is capable of manifesting ambitious plans that benefit many people, blending intuition with pragmatic execution. Under pressure its scope can overwhelm the person carrying it, and unrealized it operates more simply as the down-to-earth 4.

The 33 is the 'Master Teacher' number, a higher-octave expression of the 6 centered on selfless love, healing, and compassionate guidance for humanity. It represents the most elevated form of nurturing and service, often without the need for personal reward or recognition. It is considered rare in practice and demanding to carry, and unrealized it functions as the more ordinary caretaking energy of the 6.

systemNote: The same core or master number is read in four different positions of a numerology chart, each answering a different question. LIFE PATH — calculated from the full birth date — describes the person's overall purpose and the main lesson of the lifetime. EXPRESSION (also called DESTINY) — calculated from the full birth name — describes natural talents and abilities the person is here to develop and express. SOUL URGE (also called HEART'S DESIRE) — calculated from the vowels in the birth name — describes inner motivation, what the person deeply wants and values. PERSONAL YEAR — calculated from the birth date combined with the current calendar year — describes the theme and lessons of that specific year in a recurring nine-year cycle.

The 22 Major Arcana trace the Fool's Journey, a symbolic arc of the soul from innocence through trial to wholeness; each card represents a major archetypal force or life lesson rather than a day-to-day event (the domain of the Minor Arcana). In the tarot birth-card (or "Soul Card") system, a person's birth date is reduced to a single number (or two-digit pair) that maps to a Major Arcana card, taken to represent the core lesson or energy governing that person's life; e.g. reducing to 7 points to The Chariot, reducing to 22/4 points to The Emperor (or The Fool, in the 0-21 count), and so on depending on the reduction method used.

entries.name: The Fool
entries.meaning: The Fool represents new beginnings, innocence, and a leap into the unknown. It depicts a free spirit stepping toward a cliff's edge, trusting in the journey rather than the destination. The card signals openness to experience, spontaneity, and a willingness to take risks without over-planning. It marks the start of the Fool's Journey through the rest of the Major Arcana.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Fool warns of recklessness, naivety, or poor judgment. It can indicate being unprepared for risk, acting without thinking through consequences, or being held back by fear of taking a needed leap.

entries.name: The Magician
entries.meaning: The Magician stands for manifestation, willpower, and the resourceful use of one's skills and tools to turn ideas into reality. Traditionally shown with the four suit symbols on a table and one hand raised to the sky, one pointing to earth, he channels higher inspiration into concrete action. The card signals confidence, focus, and the ability to make things happen. It represents the point where potential becomes purposeful action.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Magician suggests manipulation, trickery, or untapped talent. It can point to poor planning, deceit, or someone using their skills for selfish or dishonest ends.

entries.name: The High Priestess
entries.meaning: The High Priestess represents intuition, the subconscious mind, and hidden or as-yet-unrevealed knowledge. Seated between two pillars, veiled from ordinary sight, she guards mysteries that must be accessed through inner reflection rather than logic. The card calls for patience, listening to inner guidance, and trusting what is felt over what is proven. It signals secrets, dreams, and knowledge not yet ready to be spoken.
entries.reversed: Reversed, she suggests disconnection from intuition, secrets coming to light, or information being withheld or misused. It can point to confusion, repressed feelings, or ignoring one's inner voice.

entries.name: The Empress
entries.meaning: The Empress embodies abundance, nurturing, fertility, and the creative force of nature. She is often shown enthroned amid lush growth, symbolizing motherhood, sensuality, and material and creative flourishing. The card signals a period of growth, comfort, and connection to the natural world or one's own creative and nurturing instincts. It can also relate literally to pregnancy, birth, or a growing project.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Empress can indicate creative block, neglect (of self or others), smothering or codependent nurturing, or issues around fertility, body image, or overindulgence.

entries.name: The Emperor
entries.meaning: The Emperor represents authority, structure, and stable, rational leadership. Seated on a stone throne, he embodies order, discipline, and the establishment of rules and boundaries that provide security. The card signals a father-figure energy: protection through structure, strategic thinking, and the will to build and defend something lasting. It favors logic and long-term planning over impulsiveness.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Emperor points to domination, rigidity, or abuse of power, or conversely a lack of discipline and structure. It can indicate an overbearing authority figure or a loss of control.

entries.name: The Hierophant
entries.meaning: The Hierophant represents tradition, established institutions, and conventional belief systems such as religion, education, or formal codes of conduct. Often depicted as a religious or spiritual teacher flanked by two acolytes, he stands for learning from recognized authorities and communal structures. The card signals conformity, seeking guidance through proven systems, and rites of passage such as marriage or formal education.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Hierophant suggests rebellion against convention, unconventional beliefs, or challenging outdated institutions. It can also indicate restriction from rigid dogma or personal, unorthodox spirituality.

entries.name: The Lovers
entries.meaning: The Lovers represents love, deep connection, and harmony between partners, as well as significant choices, especially those involving values and relationships. Traditionally depicted as a man and woman before an angel, it echoes themes of duality and union. The card signals attraction, partnership, and moments demanding a decision that aligns with one's core values.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Lovers can indicate disharmony, imbalance in a relationship, or a difficult choice made poorly or avoided. It may point to infidelity, misalignment of values, or self-love issues.

entries.name: The Chariot
entries.meaning: The Chariot represents willpower, determination, and victory achieved through focus and self-discipline. It shows a figure driving two opposing forces (often sphinxes) forward in the same direction through sheer force of will. The card signals overcoming obstacles, maintaining control amid conflicting forces, and pushing decisively toward a goal.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Chariot suggests lack of direction, loss of control, or being pulled in opposing directions. It can indicate aggression, self-doubt, or a failure to assert the will needed to move forward.

entries.name: Strength
entries.meaning: Strength represents inner fortitude, courage, and the mastery of raw impulse through patience and compassion rather than brute force. It is typically shown as a woman gently calming a lion with her bare hands, symbolizing the taming of instinct through gentleness. The card signals quiet resilience, emotional control, and facing challenges with a calm, steady heart.
entries.reversed: Reversed, Strength points to self-doubt, weakness, or a loss of inner control, sometimes expressed as raw aggression or as being overwhelmed by fear and insecurity.

entries.name: The Hermit
entries.meaning: The Hermit represents introspection, solitude, and the search for inner truth. Depicted as an old figure holding a lantern that lights the way alone on a mountain, he symbolizes withdrawal from the world to seek wisdom and self-understanding. The card signals a need for reflection, stepping back from external distractions, and following one's own inner light rather than the crowd.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Hermit suggests isolation, loneliness, or withdrawal taken too far, as well as refusing needed guidance or avoiding necessary introspection.

entries.name: Wheel of Fortune
entries.meaning: The Wheel of Fortune represents cycles, fate, and the turning points of life that lie beyond personal control. It depicts a great wheel, often with symbolic figures and alchemical or astrological symbols, illustrating the constant rise and fall of fortune. The card signals change, unexpected turns, and the reminder that both good and bad fortune are temporary phases in an ongoing cycle.
entries.reversed: Reversed, the Wheel suggests bad luck, resistance to change, or feeling like a victim of circumstance. It can also point to a cycle repeating because its lesson has not yet been learned.

entries.name: Justice
entries.meaning: Justice represents fairness, truth, and the law of cause and effect. Shown seated with a sword and scales, the figure weighs actions and their consequences impartially. The card signals accountability, the need for honesty and balance, and matters of legal or ethical decision-making where truth ultimately prevails.
entries.reversed: Reversed, Justice points to unfairness, dishonesty, or avoidance of accountability. It can indicate a biased outcome, legal complications, or consequences one is trying to escape.

entries.name: The Hanged Man
entries.meaning: The Hanged Man represents surrender, suspension, and gaining a new perspective by pausing rather than pushing forward. Depicted hanging upside down from one foot, calm rather than distressed, he symbolizes voluntary sacrifice or a change in viewpoint that brings insight. The card signals a need to let go of control, accept a period of waiting, and see a situation from a different angle.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Hanged Man suggests stalling, resistance to necessary sacrifice, or feeling stuck without gaining the intended insight. It can indicate martyrdom or delaying a decision out of fear.

entries.name: Death
entries.meaning: Death represents endings, transformation, and the transition from one state of being to another. It rarely refers to literal death, instead symbolizing the necessary conclusion of a chapter, relationship, habit, or identity to make way for renewal. The card signals profound change, release of what no longer serves, and the natural cycle of ending and rebirth.
entries.reversed: Reversed, Death suggests resistance to change, being stuck in a stagnant situation, or a fear of letting go that delays necessary transformation.

entries.name: Temperance
entries.meaning: Temperance represents balance, moderation, and the harmonious blending of opposites. Often shown as an angel pouring liquid between two cups, it symbolizes patience, careful integration, and finding the middle path. The card signals a call for calm, measured action, healing, and combining differing elements (ideas, people, or emotions) into a workable whole.
entries.reversed: Reversed, Temperance points to imbalance, excess, or impatience. It can indicate overindulgence, discord between conflicting needs, or a lack of long-term perspective.

entries.name: The Devil
entries.meaning: The Devil represents bondage, materialism, and unhealthy attachment, often to habits, desires, or relationships that limit freedom. It depicts a horned figure with two chained figures, though the chains are loose enough to be removed, suggesting the bondage is largely self-imposed. The card signals addiction, obsession, and confronting one's shadow self or destructive patterns.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Devil suggests breaking free from limiting patterns, releasing an addiction, or reclaiming personal power. It can also indicate ongoing denial about a harmful attachment.

entries.name: The Tower
entries.meaning: The Tower represents sudden, disruptive upheaval that destroys false structures and forces revelation. It depicts a tower struck by lightning, its inhabitants falling from a height, symbolizing the collapse of illusions, beliefs, or situations built on unstable foundations. The card signals a shocking event or realization that, while painful, clears the way for necessary truth and rebuilding.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Tower suggests disaster narrowly avoided, or an inevitable collapse being delayed or resisted, prolonging instability. It can also point to internal upheaval not yet outwardly expressed.

entries.name: The Star
entries.meaning: The Star represents hope, inspiration, and renewal following a period of hardship. It shows a figure pouring water under a starlit sky, symbolizing healing, spiritual replenishment, and faith in the future. The card signals optimism, calm after the storm, and a renewed sense of purpose and connection to something greater.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Star suggests despair, loss of faith, or feeling disconnected from hope and inspiration. It can indicate self-doubt or a period of discouragement following disappointment.

entries.name: The Moon
entries.meaning: The Moon represents illusion, fear, and the murky realm of the unconscious and dreams. It depicts a moonlit path between two towers, guarded by a wolf and dog, with a crayfish emerging from water, symbolizing hidden fears and instincts rising to the surface. The card signals uncertainty, confusion, and a need to trust intuition when clear answers are not available.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Moon suggests the release of fear, the resolving of confusion, or repressed emotions and hidden truths coming to light. It can also indicate ongoing self-deception or anxiety.

entries.name: The Sun
entries.meaning: The Sun represents joy, success, and vitality. It depicts a radiant sun shining over a child (often riding a horse), symbolizing clarity, warmth, and unguarded happiness. The card signals genuine positivity, achievement, good health, and a phase of life illuminated by confidence and truth.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The Sun can indicate temporary sadness, a delay in success, or an overly inflated ego. It often points to a dimming, rather than disappearance, of the card's natural optimism.

entries.name: Judgement
entries.meaning: Judgement represents reckoning, rebirth, and answering an inner call to a higher purpose. It depicts figures rising from graves at the sound of an angel's trumpet, symbolizing awakening, self-evaluation, and absolution. The card signals a pivotal moment of reflection on past actions, culminating in renewal, forgiveness, or a decisive turning point.
entries.reversed: Reversed, Judgement suggests self-doubt, harsh self-criticism, or failing to heed an inner calling. It can indicate avoidance of necessary reflection or difficulty forgiving oneself or others.

entries.name: The World
entries.meaning: The World represents completion, fulfillment, and the successful integration of a long journey's lessons. It depicts a dancing figure encircled by a laurel wreath, surrounded by the four fixed-sign symbols, signaling wholeness and accomplishment. The card marks the end of the Fool's Journey through the Major Arcana: a cycle brought to satisfying closure, ready to begin anew at a higher level.
entries.reversed: Reversed, The World suggests incompletion, delays, or a sense of something left unresolved. It can indicate a need for closure or difficulty moving on to the next stage of life.

systemNote: The 24 runes of the Elder Futhark are drawn from a pouch or cast onto a cloth and read either singly or in a small spread. Most runes can land upright or inverted (merkstave/reversed), which traditionally darkens, blocks, or delays the upright sense; a handful of runes (Gebo, Hagalaz, Isa, Jera, Ingwaz, Dagaz, and sometimes Sowilo) are symmetrical or treated as non-reversible and are read the same way regardless of orientation. This Codex records the upright divinatory meaning for each rune; reversed nuance is a matter for the interpretive layer, not this reference entry.

entries.name: Fehu
entries.meaning: Fehu represents cattle, the ancient Germanic measure of movable wealth, and signals material gain, prosperity, and resources gained through effort. It marks a time of abundance, success, or the arrival of new energy and opportunity. Because wealth in herds must be tended and can be lost, Fehu also carries a reminder that gains must be maintained and used wisely rather than hoarded.

entries.name: Uruz
entries.meaning: Uruz is named for the aurochs, a powerful and now-extinct wild ox, and represents raw physical strength, vitality, and untamed will. It speaks to endurance, good health, and the courage to face challenges head-on. The rune signals a surge of primal energy and the toughening that comes through testing and hardship.

entries.name: Thurisaz
entries.meaning: Thurisaz means thorn or giant, referencing the jötnar, and carries a charge of danger, conflict, and disruptive force. It can indicate a defensive or reactive power, such as a thorn that wounds or a barrier that protects, and often points to a difficult confrontation or a jolt that breaks a stagnant situation. The rune warns of impulsive or destructive energy that must be handled with caution.

entries.name: Ansuz
entries.meaning: Ansuz is associated with Odin and the breath or voice of the divine, representing communication, wisdom, and inspired speech. It signals messages, insight, or guidance received from a wise source, and favors learning, teaching, and persuasive or truthful communication. The rune points to the power of words and the arrival of clarifying knowledge.

entries.name: Raidho
entries.meaning: Raidho means riding or the wheeled wagon, and represents travel, journeys, and forward movement in life. It can indicate a literal trip or a more figurative progression along one's path, with emphasis on rightful order, timing, and the rhythm of a well-directed course. The rune counsels steady, correct action to reach one's destination.

entries.name: Kenaz
entries.meaning: Kenaz means torch or fire used as a tool, symbolizing controlled illumination, knowledge, and creative skill. It signals insight, learning, and the light that reveals what was hidden, as well as inspiration applied to craft or art. The rune reflects transformation through the guided use of fire, skill, and inner vision rather than destructive flame.

entries.name: Gebo
entries.meaning: Gebo means gift, representing generosity, exchange, and the balanced reciprocity between giver and receiver. It signals partnerships, contracts, and relationships built on fair give-and-take, including gifts of love, hospitality, or sacrifice. The rune is shaped as a symmetrical X and is traditionally treated as non-reversible, carrying no inverted meaning.

entries.name: Wunjo
entries.meaning: Wunjo means joy and represents harmony, contentment, and the pleasure of shared fellowship. It signals a period of happiness, success, and smooth cooperation with others, often after hardship has passed. The rune reflects emotional well-being and the comfort of belonging to a community or kinship group.

entries.name: Hagalaz
entries.meaning: Hagalaz means hail, the destructive ice that falls from the sky and then melts away, symbolizing sudden disruption and uncontrollable natural force. It represents crisis, hardship, or upheaval that is beyond one's control but ultimately temporary and often clears the way for renewal. The rune is treated as non-reversible, since hail's disruptive nature is constant regardless of orientation.

entries.name: Nauthiz
entries.meaning: Nauthiz means need and represents constraint, hardship, and the friction of necessity. It signals resistance, delay, or want, but also the resilience and resourcefulness that emerge from being tested by difficult circumstance. The rune counsels patience and self-reliance in the face of restriction.

entries.name: Isa
entries.meaning: Isa means ice, symbolizing stillness, stasis, and a freeze on activity or progress. It signals a period where action is blocked or must be suspended, calling for patience, caution, and clear-headed waiting rather than forcing movement. As a single unbroken vertical stroke, Isa is treated as non-reversible.

entries.name: Jera
entries.meaning: Jera means year or harvest, representing the natural cycle of sowing and reaping and the reward that comes from sustained effort. It signals fruition, positive change, and the fair completion of a cycle in its proper season. The rune reflects the working of natural law and is treated as non-reversible due to its symmetrical form.

entries.name: Eihwaz
entries.meaning: Eihwaz is linked to the yew tree, a long-lived and poisonous tree associated with both death and endurance, and represents resilience, protection, and steady transformation. It signals a dependable foundation, defense against hardship, and the capacity to endure through change or ending. The rune reflects the link between mortality and continuity, marking transitions that strengthen rather than destroy.

entries.name: Perthro
entries.meaning: Perthro's exact meaning is debated but is traditionally linked to a dice-cup or container used for casting lots, representing chance, fate, and hidden or unknown outcomes. It signals mysteries, secrets, and matters governed by luck or destiny rather than direct control. The rune is associated with divination itself and with the revealing of what is presently concealed.

entries.name: Algiz
entries.meaning: Algiz, shaped like an outstretched hand or elk's antlers, represents protection, defense, and a shielding or warding force. It signals guardianship, vigilance against danger, and connection to higher or spiritual guidance that watches over the individual. The rune counsels alertness and invokes safety in times of vulnerability.

entries.name: Sowilo
entries.meaning: Sowilo means sun and represents success, vitality, and life-giving energy. It signals victory, good health, and clear guidance, much as the sun lights the way and sustains life. The rune reflects wholeness, achievement, and positive momentum reaching its goal.

entries.name: Tiwaz
entries.meaning: Tiwaz is named for Tyr, the god of law and justice who sacrificed his hand to bind the wolf Fenrir, and represents honor, justice, and disciplined self-sacrifice. It signals fair victory earned through duty, courage, and adherence to principle rather than force alone. The rune favors leadership, right conduct, and standing firm for what is just.

entries.name: Berkano
entries.meaning: Berkano is named for the birch tree, one of the first to grow leaves in spring, and represents fertility, growth, and new beginnings. It signals nurturing energy, family matters, birth, and the careful tending needed for something new to flourish. The rune reflects the feminine, protective, and regenerative forces of nature.

entries.name: Ehwaz
entries.meaning: Ehwaz means horse, symbolizing the close working partnership between horse and rider and, by extension, trust, teamwork, and cooperative progress. It signals beneficial movement forward, loyal relationships, and steady advancement made possible through mutual reliance. The rune favors harmony between partners working toward a shared goal.

entries.name: Mannaz
entries.meaning: Mannaz means man or humankind, representing the self in relation to others and the shared nature of human society. It signals self-awareness, intelligence, and the social bonds, structures, and cooperation that support communal life. The rune reflects one's place within the human order and the balance between individual identity and collective belonging.

entries.name: Laguz
entries.meaning: Laguz means water or lake, representing intuition, emotion, and the fluid, unconscious currents of life. It signals psychic sensitivity, imagination, and the need to flow with circumstances rather than resist them. The rune reflects the deep, sometimes hidden emotional undercurrents that guide instinct and inner life.

entries.name: Ingwaz
entries.meaning: Ingwaz is named for Ing, an early form of the god Freyr associated with fertility and peace, and represents gestation, stored potential, and internal growth building toward release. It signals a phase of quiet preparation or incubation before a goal reaches completion and manifests outwardly. The rune's symmetrical diamond form is treated as non-reversible.

entries.name: Dagaz
entries.meaning: Dagaz means day, representing the breakthrough of dawn light and a decisive, positive transformation. It signals awakening, clarity, and a turning point where a new and better phase begins, often bringing sudden and welcome change. The rune's symmetrical form is treated as non-reversible, reflecting its unambiguous, wholly positive charge.

entries.name: Othala
entries.meaning: Othala means ancestral property or homeland, representing inherited wealth, family legacy, and one's roots or homeland. It signals the values, traditions, and belongings passed down through generations, along with a sense of stable belonging and identity tied to home and kin. The rune favors matters of inheritance, land, and the enduring foundation provided by one's heritage.

systemNote: The eight trigrams (bagua) are the three-line building blocks of the I Ching. Each hexagram is formed by stacking two trigrams — a lower (inner) and an upper (outer) trigram. Each trigram carries a natural image, a family role (father, mother, and three sons and three daughters, ranked by which line differs from the parent trigram), and a core attribute that colors any hexagram it appears in.

entries.name: ☰ Qián · Heaven
entries.tag: The Creative · yang · the father · attribute: strong
entries.meaning: Qian is composed of three unbroken lines, pure yang. Its image is Heaven, and its attribute is strength without pause — untiring, persistent movement. In the family it is the father, the originating, generative power. Wherever it appears it drives a hexagram's energy outward and upward, initiating rather than responding.

entries.name: ☷ Kūn · Earth
entries.tag: The Receptive · yin · the mother · attribute: devoted, yielding
entries.meaning: Kun is composed of three broken lines, pure yin. Its image is Earth, which carries and nourishes all things without competing with them. In the family it is the mother, the complementary power to Qian: where Heaven initiates, Earth completes and sustains. Its attribute is devotion — yielding strength that supports through receiving rather than forcing.

entries.name: ☳ Zhèn · Thunder
entries.tag: The Arousing · yang below two yin · the eldest son · attribute: movement, shock
entries.meaning: Zhen has one strong line below two yielding lines, the yang pushing up from beneath. Its image is Thunder — sudden movement that startles and arouses. In the family it is the eldest son, the first to inherit the father's initiating power. Its attribute is shock followed by movement: it breaks stillness and sets things going, often bringing initial alarm that clears into action.

entries.name: ☵ Kǎn · Water
entries.tag: The Abysmal · yang between two yin · the middle son · attribute: danger, depth
entries.meaning: Kan has one strong line enclosed between two yielding lines, image of water held within a hollow. Its image is Water — specifically water in a ravine or repeated peril, flowing constantly toward its goal despite obstacles. In the family it is the middle son. Its attribute is danger: a hexagram touched by Kan carries genuine risk, but also water's persistence in always finding its course.

entries.name: ☶ Gèn · Mountain
entries.tag: Keeping Still · yang above two yin · the youngest son · attribute: stillness, rest
entries.meaning: Gen has one strong line resting atop two yielding lines, image of a solid cap that halts what lies beneath. Its image is Mountain — motionless, a boundary marking where movement stops. In the family it is the youngest son. Its attribute is stillness: knowing when to stop, holding firm, and providing the rest that gives later movement its meaning.

entries.name: ☴ Xùn · Wind/Wood
entries.tag: The Gentle · yin below two yang · the eldest daughter · attribute: penetration, gradual influence
entries.meaning: Xun has one yielding line below two strong lines, the yin entering from beneath. Its image is Wind (also Wood) — invisible, penetrating, and unable to be blocked outright, working its way gradually into every gap. In the family it is the eldest daughter. Its attribute is gentle penetration: influence that succeeds not by force but by patient, continuous, adaptable entry.

entries.name: ☲ Lí · Fire
entries.tag: The Clinging · yin between two yang · the middle daughter · attribute: clarity, dependence
entries.meaning: Li has one yielding line enclosed between two strong lines, image of flame that clings to fuel in order to burn. Its image is Fire, and also the Sun — brightness, clarity, and illumination. In the family it is the middle daughter. Its attribute is clinging: like fire it has no independent substance of its own but lights up whatever it attaches to, representing intelligence, awareness, and dependence on something to illuminate.

entries.name: ☱ Duì · Lake
entries.tag: The Joyous · yin above two yang · the youngest daughter · attribute: joy, openness
entries.meaning: Dui has one yielding line resting atop two strong lines, the open yin surface above firm yang beneath. Its image is Lake — a still, open body of water that reflects and gives nourishment, and also the mouth, associated with speech and pleasure. In the family it is the youngest daughter. Its attribute is joy: cheerful, satisfying, sociable expression that arises when inner firmness meets an open, yielding surface.

systemNote: The 64 hexagrams in the King Wen sequence, Wilhelm-Baynes names. Each is two trigrams stacked (upper over lower). A reading casts one hexagram; moving lines can turn it into a second, becoming-hexagram. The Codex gives the primary sense of each figure.

entries.name: 1 · The Creative (Qián)
entries.meaning: Pure yang energy, doubled: unyielding strength and tireless creative force. It counsels sustained effort, firm perseverance, and the initiative to begin great undertakings. Success comes through steady, disciplined application of strength, not through force alone. The superior person is urged to model themselves on heaven's ceaseless motion, strengthening themselves without interruption.

entries.name: 2 · The Receptive (Kūn)
entries.meaning: Pure yin energy, doubled: complete receptivity, devotion, and the power to sustain and nourish. Where the Creative initiates, the Receptive completes and carries through, gaining its strength through yielding rather than resisting. It counsels following rather than leading, gentleness, and quiet strength like the earth that bears all things. Advantage comes from finding the right guidance and remaining steadfast in support of it.

entries.name: 3 · Difficulty at the Beginning (Zhūn)
entries.meaning: A young plant pushing through hard earth: the confusion and difficulty that accompany any true beginning. Energy is present but not yet organized, so obstacles abound and the way forward is unclear. It counsels not forcing a resolution alone but seeking capable helpers, remaining firm, and consolidating small gains rather than rushing. Great progress is possible, but only through patient effort at the outset.

entries.name: 4 · Youthful Folly (Méng)
entries.meaning: A spring emerging at the foot of a mountain, not yet finding its course: the state of inexperience and the need for instruction. Success comes not from the teacher seeking the student, but from the student's sincere desire to learn; asking repeatedly or disrespectfully clouds the answer. It counsels patient, firm, and timely instruction, and a willingness to remain teachable rather than reckless.

entries.name: 5 · Waiting (Xū)
entries.meaning: Clouds gathered in the sky, rain not yet fallen: a time when strength is present but the moment for action has not yet arrived. It counsels calm confidence, using the delay to nourish body and spirit rather than forcing events. Danger lies ahead, but one who waits in sincerity and does not act rashly will cross through safely when the time comes.

entries.name: 6 · Conflict (Sòng)
entries.meaning: Heaven moving upward and water flowing downward, two forces working in opposing directions: the image of contention. It warns that even a just cause pursued through conflict brings danger, and that pushing a dispute to the end brings misfortune. The counsel is to seek mediation, to be willing to stop halfway, and to avoid entanglement in prolonged struggle even when one holds the right.

entries.name: 7 · The Army (Shī)
entries.meaning: Water gathered beneath the earth, hidden masses held in reserve: the image of a disciplined army led by a firm, experienced commander. Great undertakings involving many people require unified command, clear discipline, and a just cause to succeed. It counsels that strength must be organized and restrained by rule of law, used only when necessary, and directed by a mature leader who bears responsibility for those under them.

entries.name: 8 · Holding Together (Bǐ)
entries.meaning: Water resting on the earth, flowing to fill every low place without being forced: the image of natural union and mutual support. It counsels seeking closeness and alliance with others based on inner sincerity, and doing so promptly, since those who hesitate too long lose the chance to join. Union must have a worthy center to gather around, or the joining together is empty and brings misfortune.

entries.name: 9 · The Taming Power of the Small (Xiǎo Chù)
entries.meaning: A gentle wind moving across the sky, restraining great force through gentle, persistent influence rather than direct confrontation. Only small measures of restraint are possible now; the situation calls for patience and refinement of inner character rather than dramatic action. It counsels accumulating small gains through gentleness, since the time is not yet ripe for decisive action or open advance.

entries.name: 10 · Treading (Lǚ)
entries.meaning: A lake beneath the vast sky, the image of the small and yielding treading carefully behind the strong: correct conduct in a precarious situation. Like treading on a tiger's tail, one proceeds through danger safely by remaining courteous, mindful, and correct in behavior rather than provoking what is powerful. Good fortune comes to those whose conduct is cheerful and unassuming despite the risk around them.

entries.name: 11 · Peace (Tài)
entries.meaning: Heaven's energy descending and earth's energy rising to meet it: a true and fruitful exchange between forces, producing harmony and growth. It represents a high point of prosperity, when leaders and followers, or heaven and earth, are in accord and small things depart while great things approach. Even in this favorable time, it counsels remembering that peace can turn to stagnation, and urges continued care to preserve what has been achieved.

entries.name: 12 · Standstill [Stagnation] (Pǐ)
entries.meaning: Heaven rising away and earth sinking further down, forces moving apart rather than meeting: a time of blockage, misunderstanding, and stagnation between high and low. Communication fails and the superior person's way is obstructed, so it counsels withdrawing from public engagement, conserving strength, and not associating openly with those unfit for the time. Perseverance through this barren period prepares the ground for its eventual turn.

entries.name: 13 · Fellowship with Men (Tóng Rén)
entries.meaning: Fire rising to join with heaven's light, the image of clarity joined with strength in genuine fellowship. True community is formed not through private cliques or narrow factions but through open-hearted union around a clear, impartial principle that all can share. It counsels seeking fellowship in the wide field rather than the inner chamber, so that cooperation is honest and free of hidden agendas.

entries.name: 14 · Possession in Great Measure (Dà Yǒu)
entries.meaning: The sun blazing in the height of heaven, illuminating all things: an image of great abundance and possession held at its peak. Such supreme power and wealth call for humility, generosity, and clear discernment so that abundance is used to benefit others rather than corrupt the holder. Sustained good fortune depends on restraining pride and keeping harmony with what is right despite great strength.

entries.name: 15 · Modesty (Qiān)
entries.meaning: A great mountain concealed beneath the earth, strength that does not flaunt itself: the image of true modesty, which is genuine strength held in reserve. This hexagram is unusual in being favorable in every one of its lines, since modesty rightly practiced brings success in all circumstances. It counsels leveling what is too high and raising what is too low, maintaining equilibrium through quiet, unassuming virtue rather than display.

entries.name: 16 · Enthusiasm (Yù)
entries.meaning: Thunder bursting forth from the earth, stirring movement and joyful energy: the image of enthusiasm that arises naturally and moves others to willing devotion. When leadership acts in accord with what is right and natural, others follow readily and without compulsion, much as movement conforms effortlessly to natural law. It counsels using this uplifting energy to prepare and organize — for defense, for music, for shared purpose — while staying alert that enthusiasm does not slide into excess or delusion.

entries.name: 17 · Following (Suí)
entries.meaning: The strong movement of thunder yields and settles beneath the joyous lake, teaching that true leadership means adapting to the needs of the time and the people one leads. To gain willing followers, one must first be willing to follow what is right. Success comes through flexibility, sincerity, and knowing when to accommodate rather than force.

entries.name: 18 · Work on What Has Been Spoiled (Gǔ)
entries.meaning: Stagnation and complacency have allowed corruption or neglect to set in, symbolized by wind trapped and rotting under a mountain. The figure calls for decisive work to repair what has decayed, requiring examination of past mistakes before new order can be restored. It counsels careful deliberation before acting, since remedying long-standing trouble demands patience.

entries.name: 19 · Approach (Lín)
entries.meaning: Two strong lines advance from below beneath four yielding lines, depicting a time of increasing influence and favorable growth, like the approach of spring. Those in authority are advised to draw near to their people with care and encouragement rather than force. The figure warns that prosperity brings responsibility, and that what advances will also, in time, recede.

entries.name: 20 · Contemplation (Guān)
entries.meaning: Wind moving over the earth reaches everywhere, symbolizing the quiet, far-reaching power of observation and contemplation. A ruler or person of standing influences others not through force but by the example of their own inner cultivation, viewed and absorbed as wind touches every blade of grass. It counsels stepping back to see one's situation clearly before acting.

entries.name: 21 · Biting Through (Shì Kè)
entries.meaning: Something intervenes between the upper and lower jaws, like an obstacle blocking union or order, and it must be bitten through decisively. The figure calls for clear-eyed, firm action against obstruction, dishonesty, or injustice, with the understanding that force must be paired with clarity, as fire illuminates what thunder's shock must strike. Success comes to those who confront the problem directly rather than avoid it.

entries.name: 22 · Grace (Bì)
entries.meaning: Fire's light plays beneath the still mountain, giving beautiful form and clarity to what is solid, symbolizing grace, adornment, and the refinement of outward appearance. The figure favors small, pleasing matters and social forms but warns that grace is a beautifying addition, not a substitute for substance. Genuine progress requires that inner content not be sacrificed for outer polish.

entries.name: 23 · Splitting Apart (Bō)
entries.meaning: Five yielding lines rise beneath a single strong line at the top, like a mountain whose base is crumbling into the earth, depicting a time of deterioration when inferior forces are overtaking the good. The figure counsels against futile resistance and instead urges retreat, patience, and careful timing until the situation turns. It reassures that decay, once complete, prepares the ground for renewal.

entries.name: 24 · Return (The Turning Point) (Fù)
entries.meaning: A single strong line returns at the bottom beneath five yielding lines, like the winter solstice when light begins its quiet return after the deepest dark. The figure marks a turning point after decline, where the way back to what is right opens naturally and without strain. It counsels rest and consolidation now, since the new growth is fragile and must not be forced.

entries.name: 25 · Innocence (The Unexpected) (Wú Wàng)
entries.meaning: Thunder moves freely under heaven, symbolizing action that is spontaneous, sincere, and free of ulterior motive. Great success comes to one who acts in accord with their innate, uncalculated nature rather than through scheming or self-interest. The figure warns that any deviation from what is correct, however small, invites misfortune, since innocence cannot be feigned.

entries.name: 26 · The Taming Power of the Great (Dà Xù)
entries.meaning: The mountain holds heaven's creative power within it, symbolizing great strength that is restrained, cultivated, and stored rather than spent at once. The figure favors firm containment of energy through discipline and the study of past experience, building capacity for a great undertaking. What is held back now gathers force for a crossing of great waters later.

entries.name: 27 · The Corners of the Mouth (Providing Nourishment) (Yí)
entries.meaning: The figure's open shape resembles a mouth, and its lines depict the movement of jaws, symbolizing the giving and receiving of nourishment in food, words, and ideas. It counsels careful attention to what one takes in and puts out, since both physical sustenance and speech shape one's character. True nourishment of others begins with rightly nourishing oneself.

entries.name: 28 · Preponderance of the Great (Dà Guò)
entries.meaning: A ridgepole sags under too great a load, four strong lines crowded between weak lines at top and bottom, depicting a situation of extraordinary strain that exceeds normal capacity. The figure signals a critical, exceptional time demanding equally exceptional and sometimes solitary action to prevent collapse. It counsels careful, resolute handling of an overloaded situation before it breaks.

entries.name: 29 · The Abysmal (Water) (Kǎn)
entries.meaning: Water doubled falls into every hollow it meets yet keeps flowing onward, symbolizing repeated danger and the discipline needed to move through it without losing one's course. The figure counsels facing peril with sincerity, staying true to one's inner nature the way water stays true to its own level. Danger mastered through consistent, honest effort becomes a habit that carries one safely through.

entries.name: 30 · The Clinging, Fire (Lí)
entries.meaning: Fire has no independent form of its own; it clings to what it burns, doubled here to signal brightness that depends on something to attach to for continuance. The figure calls for cultivating clarity of mind and clinging to what is correct and nourishing, as flame clings to fuel. Sustained brilliance requires steady maintenance, not a single bright flare.

entries.name: 31 · Influence (Wooing) (Xián)
entries.meaning: The lake rests above the still mountain, and the weaker element sits above the strong, depicting mutual attraction and influence exchanged freely between two open, receptive parties. The figure favors success in relationships and undertakings built on genuine, unforced responsiveness rather than domination. It marks the beginning of a new cycle of union, following naturally after the hexagrams of heaven and earth's foundational order.

entries.name: 32 · Duration (Héng)
entries.meaning: Thunder and wind reinforce each other in continuous, regular motion, symbolizing duration and the capacity to endure by adapting within a stable, unchanging purpose. Unlike static permanence, true duration comes from consistent movement along a fixed course, as a marriage endures through changing seasons of life. The figure counsels perseverance in one's proper direction rather than clinging rigidly to any single circumstance.

entries.name: 33 · Retreat (Dùn)
entries.meaning: The mountain rises toward heaven but heaven withdraws before it, an image of orderly retreat while strength is still intact. Retreat here is not defeat but wisdom: the superior person disengages from a worsening situation early and with dignity, rather than clinging until forced out. Small-mindedness is advancing, so the wise course is to yield ground calmly, preserving strength for a better time.

entries.name: 34 · The Power of the Great (Dà Zhuàng)
entries.meaning: Thunder sounds above heaven, a picture of great power in vigorous motion. Genuine strength must be paired with correctness and restraint, for power used without regard for what is right soon exhausts or destroys itself. The superior person does not tread on paths that are not proper, using strength only in accord with what is right.

entries.name: 35 · Progress (Jìn)
entries.meaning: The sun rises over the earth, an image of rapid, illuminating advance. This hexagram describes swift progress and increasing recognition, as when a capable person is welcomed and advanced by a ruler or superior. The counsel is to let one's inner light shine forth of itself, advancing openly and without forcing the pace.

entries.name: 36 · Darkening of the Light (Míng Yí)
entries.meaning: The light of the sun has sunk beneath the earth, an image of brightness injured and obscured. In times when darkness and injustice prevail, one must veil one's own light to avoid danger, maintaining inner clarity and firm purpose despite outward difficulty. It counsels quiet perseverance and concealment of one's true worth until the danger passes.

entries.name: 37 · The Family (Jiā Rén)
entries.meaning: Wind rising from fire suggests the outward influence generated from within the home. This hexagram concerns the proper ordering of the family, in which each member's clear role and conduct radiate outward to steady the wider community. Firm, loving discipline within the household is the model and foundation for order beyond it.

entries.name: 38 · Opposition (Kuí)
entries.meaning: Fire flares upward while the lake's waters seep downward, two natures moving apart even as they touch, an image of opposition and estrangement. Misunderstanding and divergence of viewpoint separate people who nonetheless share some common ground, so that great undertakings must be postponed while small matters may still succeed. The counsel is to seek limited points of agreement rather than forcing harmony where fundamental differences remain.

entries.name: 39 · Obstruction (Jiǎn)
entries.meaning: Water lies on top of the mountain, a dangerous abyss blocking further advance, an image of obstruction and hardship. Progress forward is genuinely blocked, and the proper response is not to force one's way through but to pause, turn inward to examine oneself, and seek the aid of capable friends or allies. Through patience and inner cultivation, the obstruction in time gives way.

entries.name: 40 · Deliverance (Xiè)
entries.meaning: Thunder and rain break together, releasing the tension that has built in the atmosphere, an image of deliverance from difficulty. A period of obstruction is coming to an end, and the accumulated pressure is relieved, calling for prompt action to consolidate the relief before new complications form. It counsels forgiving faults and errors once the danger has passed, and moving decisively to set things right.

entries.name: 41 · Decrease (Sǔn)
entries.meaning: The lake at the foot of the mountain diminishes itself as it evaporates upward to nourish the height, an image of decrease that serves a higher purpose. Decreasing what is below to increase what is above, or trimming excess in oneself in favor of simplicity and sincerity, can be beneficial if done at the right time and in the right measure. What matters is not the quantity given up but the genuineness of spirit behind the sacrifice.

entries.name: 42 · Increase (Yì)
entries.meaning: Wind and thunder reinforce each other, each intensifying the other's effect, an image of mutual increase. This hexagram favors undertakings and forward movement, describing a time when those above genuinely benefit those below, and resources or energy flow downward to nourish growth. It is an auspicious moment to act, to correct faults, and to press ahead while the current of increase is running.

entries.name: 43 · Breakthrough (Guài)
entries.meaning: The lake has risen to the very top of heaven, about to overflow, an image of a critical mass about to break through. A single small, corrupting element remains at the top of an otherwise strong situation, and it must be resolutely and openly exposed and removed, though not through violence or anger. The counsel is firmness joined with fairness, announcing the matter openly rather than resorting to force.

entries.name: 44 · Coming to Meet (Gòu)
entries.meaning: Under heaven, wind moves everywhere, touching all things, an image of an unforeseen encounter that quietly permeates a situation. A single yielding or dubious element has arisen from below and begins to gain influence, and though it may seem harmless or attractive at first, unchecked it can undermine what is strong. The counsel is vigilance, keeping such an influence in check rather than granting it power.

entries.name: 45 · Gathering Together (Cuì)
entries.meaning: The lake gathers upon the earth, its waters collecting into one body, an image of people or forces gathering together. This hexagram concerns the assembly of a community or following around a strong, worthy leader, sustained through sincerity, offerings, and shared ritual observance. Great achievements become possible when people are drawn together in genuine unity, but vigilance is needed against disorder within the gathered mass.

entries.name: 46 · Pushing Upward (Shēng)
entries.meaning: Wind, or wood, grows quietly upward within the earth, pushing steadily toward the light, an image of gradual, organic ascent. Advancement here comes not through force but through steady, modest effort that accumulates naturally over time, like a plant pushing up through soil. Seeking the counsel of the great and pressing onward without anxiety brings good fortune as this patient rise continues.

entries.name: 47 · Oppression (Kùn)
entries.meaning: The lake has drained away its water, leaving itself dry and exhausted, an image of oppression and depletion. Outer circumstances are constrained and difficult, resources are scarce, and words or complaints may go unheeded, so that inner fortitude and quiet composure become the only real recourse. The superior person accepts the hardship without losing resolve, maintaining cheerfulness and correctness until the constraint lifts.

entries.name: 48 · The Well (Jǐng)
entries.meaning: Water rests above wood, or a bucket draws water up through a wooden well-shaft, an image of a communal well that nourishes all who come to it. The well itself remains constant even as towns are built and rebuilt around it, symbolizing an inexhaustible source of nourishment that must nonetheless be kept clean, deep, and properly maintained to be of use. Neglect of the rope or a cracked jug spoils the benefit, so the counsel is diligent care of the source that sustains everyone.

entries.name: 49 · Revolution (Gé)
entries.meaning: Fire beneath, lake above: the two mutually extinguishing or transforming elements depict fundamental change, like the moulting of an animal's skin. Revolution is necessary when old forms no longer serve, but it succeeds only when the timing is right and the change is grounded in truth, not mere agitation. Once accomplished, it must be consolidated and trusted, as regret disappears when the new order proves itself.

entries.name: 50 · The Cauldron (Dǐng)
entries.meaning: Wind and fire together feed a flame beneath a ritual vessel, symbolizing the transformation of raw material into nourishment and, by extension, the cultivation of talent and character. The cauldron stands for stability, sustenance, and the sacred rites that bind a community. It counsels using one's resources to nourish others and to refine what is crude into what is of worth.

entries.name: 51 · The Arousing, Shock (Zhèn)
entries.meaning: Thunder doubled represents shock that comes suddenly, terrifying at first but ultimately clarifying. The superior person meets shock with inner composure, using the fright to review their conduct and cultivate reverent self-examination. What alarms from without need not disturb what is settled within, and the ability to remain steady amid upheaval is itself the lesson.

entries.name: 52 · Keeping Still, Mountain (Gèn)
entries.meaning: The mountain, doubled, stands motionless, symbolizing a stillness that is deliberate rather than passive: knowing when to stop and rest content within one's own place. True stillness is achieved by keeping the back at rest, disregarding external distraction, so the self is not disturbed. This hexagram counsels meditative quiet, clear boundaries, and freedom from restless desire.

entries.name: 53 · Development, Gradual Progress (Jiàn)
entries.meaning: A tree growing slowly on a mountain, its roots deepening as it rises, depicts development that proceeds step by step in the correct order, as in the traditional stages of courtship and marriage. Advance that is gradual and follows proper form endures, while haste undermines its own aims. The image counsels patient, well-ordered progress toward a distant goal.

entries.name: 54 · The Marrying Maiden (Guī Mèi)
entries.meaning: A younger woman entering marriage as a secondary wife represents a relationship or venture entered from a subordinate or irregular position, requiring especial care in conduct. Impulsive or improper unions bring misfortune, while acting with modesty, patience, and awareness of one's actual standing brings a workable outcome. The hexagram warns against forcing a situation that lacks a firm, correct foundation.

entries.name: 55 · Abundance, Fullness (Fēng)
entries.meaning: Thunder and lightning together depict a moment of great abundance and clarity, when brilliance of insight is joined to vigorous action, as at the sun's zenith. Such fullness is inherently transient, and the wise ruler enjoys it without anxiety while remaining mindful that all high points decline. The counsel is to act decisively and generously in the time of plenty, without clinging to it.

entries.name: 56 · The Wanderer (Lǚ)
entries.meaning: Fire atop a motionless mountain, spreading without a fixed base, portrays the condition of a traveler or stranger without settled roots. The wanderer must be cautious, adaptable, and correct in conduct, seeking only modest, temporary lodging rather than grand aims, since a stranger commands no lasting authority. Success in this state comes through clarity of purpose and care in small matters.

entries.name: 57 · The Gentle, Penetrating, Wind (Xùn)
entries.meaning: Wind doubled penetrates everywhere gradually and without force, symbolizing influence that succeeds through gentle, persistent, repeated application rather than confrontation. Effective action here follows clear orders quietly carried out, adjusting to circumstance while holding to a firm, small-scale center. It teaches that lasting change comes from unassuming, continuous pressure, not sudden assertion.

entries.name: 58 · The Joyous, Lake (Duì)
entries.meaning: Two lakes joined, each replenishing the other, represent joy that arises from openness, mutual encouragement, and genuine communication between people. True joy is firm and sincere within, expressed outwardly in a way that stimulates and pleases others, as opposed to shallow or seductive pleasure. The image counsels cultivating friendship and cheerful exchange grounded in inner strength.

entries.name: 59 · Dispersion, Dissolution (Huàn)
entries.meaning: Wind moving over water scatters and dissolves it into spray, depicting the dispersal of stagnation, rigid separation, or selfish division within a group or the self. Rulers or leaders address this by gathering people through shared ritual and higher purpose, dissolving hardened factions into renewed unity. The counsel is to actively dissolve what has congealed into isolation, restoring connection.

entries.name: 60 · Limitation (Jié)
entries.meaning: Water contained within a lake gives the lake its form, showing that appropriate limitation gives structure and usefulness to what would otherwise dissipate. Limitation must be set with moderation, since limits that are too severe become a source of hardship rather than order. The wise establish standards and measures suited to circumstance, disciplining themselves without excessive austerity.

entries.name: 61 · Inner Truth (Zhōng Fú)
entries.meaning: Wind moving over the open lake stirs it to its depths, symbolizing a sincerity so complete it reaches and moves even those far removed or fundamentally different, like fish or pigs. Genuine inner truth, held with an open and unprejudiced heart, communicates itself without need for outward compulsion. This hexagram counsels utmost honesty and empathetic understanding as the root of true influence.

entries.name: 62 · Preponderance of the Small (Xiǎo Guò)
entries.meaning: Thunder on a mountain depicts an exceptional situation in which small, careful, attentive action is fitting, while great or ambitious undertakings should be avoided. Success comes through modesty, precision in small affairs, and remaining close to the matter at hand rather than reaching too high or too far. It counsels conscientious restraint until conditions allow for larger action.

entries.name: 63 · After Completion (Jì Jì)
entries.meaning: Water above fire, each in its proper place, portrays a state in which order has been achieved and all is, for the moment, well arranged. Yet perfect order is inherently unstable and tends toward its opposite, so the superior person remains vigilant, anticipating decline and guarding carefully against disorder even at the height of success. It is a caution against complacency once a goal is reached.

entries.name: 64 · Before Completion (Wèi Jì)
entries.meaning: Fire above water, each element out of its natural place and straining toward the other, depicts a situation of transition on the verge of resolution but not yet accomplished. Success is possible if one proceeds with caution, discrimination, and correct order, like a fox crossing ice who must test each step. The hexagram closes the sequence on the promise of new order emerging from careful, deliberate completion of what remains unfinished.

systemNote: Unlike the omen systems catalogued elsewhere in the Codex, this bank is native to the Blue Room — its own working parts rather than borrowed symbols: the panels that read a frame, the surfaces that hold its material, and the plates that file its record. Every entry here reads the artifact — the photograph, the print, the edition, the card — and never the person who stands in it, and no entry ranks one card, or one person, above another.

entries.name: Metrics
entries.meaning: Metrics is one of the three tabs on the left panel, a register reel of Diagnostic Receipts turned one plate at a time. Each plate reads the frame as a whole — Frame Signature as a silhouette rather than a score, Signal Mix as a proportion of the whole, Composition Field as a drift node set against tier bands, Frame Event as the recorded act with its bands. It is an interpretive read of the artifact's own material and may carry the material's accent colour; it is never a measure of any person and never resolves to a number on a scale.

entries.name: Diagram
entries.meaning: The Diagram is the silver-instrument figure: the photograph laid on a scan sheet beneath measured overlays, always annotated. Grid, mass, focal point, gesture line, axis, arrows, and light are drawn across the frame and named with labels. It is rendered in silver rather than the card's material, and it reads the geometry of the frame — where weight falls and how the eye is led — not the figure standing inside it.

entries.name: Source
entries.meaning: The Source is the original photograph, kept whole and clean with no overlays laid across it. It opens on its own in a lightbox at plain size. This is the unaltered input from which the entire card is developed, held apart so the raw frame can be seen exactly as it was taken in — the plate every other instrument works from.

entries.name: Surface
entries.meaning: Surface is the right-panel module that fuses the Finish with the Colour Field. The palette is sampled straight off the photograph's surface and drawn as a proportion bar, capped by the material chip and carried alongside the finish name with a short character note. It records the card's material identity — how the print sits and reflects — read from the surface itself, never anything about its subject.

entries.name: The Developed Back
entries.tag: the sealed back · a mode of the same card
entries.meaning: In the free state the card front is already complete; developing opens the sealed back of that same card. The back carries the dossier plates — Surface Record, Archetype, Aura, Mint Record, and the Oracle Read — as added depth rather than a higher tier, drawn from the photograph already on file. It is a mode entered on the existing artifact: the same card, turned over and developed, with no new card made in the turning.

entries.name: Surface Record
entries.meaning: Surface Record is the first plate of the developed back, the photograph's palette rendered as live pigment suspended in developer fluid across a horizontal tray. Each colour lies in the bath as its own proportion of the whole. It records the colour provenance of the print — which pigments the surface was drawn from and in what measure — and nothing of the person pictured.

entries.name: Mint Record
entries.meaning: Mint Record is the fourth plate of the developed back, the edition and serial provenance of the card as a filed object. It sets down the object number, the edition of one of one, the serial, and the marks of a thing filed, sealed, and recorded — the act of committing the card to your own private archive, a record and not a transaction. Each mint is a record; each record is yours, reachable through a QR panel marked Access Mint.
